bull_thesis: MYR Group is a pure-play physical contractor for the two most bottlenecked layers of the AI build-out: high-voltage grid interconnection (transmission and distribution, or T&D) and mission-critical data-center electrical fit-out (commercial and industrial, or C&I). The fundamental trajectory is unambiguously strengthening: net income went from $30.3 million in fiscal 2024 to $118.4 million in fiscal 2025 to $46.8 million in the first quarter of 2026 alone; earnings per share went from $1.83 to $7.53 to $2.99 per quarter; gross margin expanded from 8.6% to 11.6% to 13.4%; and the backlog reached a record $2.84 billion. In Q1 2026 management raised both the full-year revenue guidance (from 10% to 12% growth) and both segment operating-margin bands (T&D to 8–11%, C&I to 6–9%) — a double raise, not a beat-and-hold. The demand is quantified and structural: data-center construction starts are up nearly 100% year-over-year (per CEO Egan), large transmission awards covering 345 kV lines and substations in South Carolina, Arizona, and Texas are booking revenue 'in 2027 or later,' and the CEO frames T&D as a decade-long nationwide build rather than a localized boom like the 2013–15 CREZ (Competitive Renewable Energy Zone) cycle in Texas. The balance sheet is pristine — $163 million in cash, $9 million in funded debt, 0.04 times leverage, and $230 million in trailing-twelve-month free cash flow — giving real optionality for acquisitions or buybacks. Return on equity is approximately 22.7% and return on invested capital approximately 15.5%, both above the company's cost of capital and rising. Master service agreements (MSAs — long-term preferred-contractor arrangements) now account for roughly 70% of T&D revenue, improving the recurring quality of earnings. Management is disciplined ('selective on larger projects,' 'don't want to be first in') and pre-discloses friction (days-sales-outstanding drift). This is the picks-and-shovels electrical contractor that every grid and data-center capital-expenditure dollar must pay, run by a credible and improving team with a fortress balance sheet. The thesis holds as long as AI and electrification capital spending stays durable, large transmission awards convert to backlog in the second half of 2026 and into 2027, and margins hold near the raised guidance bands.
bear_thesis: The business is excellent; the stock is the problem. MYR Group is up 176% over the past 52 weeks to roughly $461, trading at approximately 50 times trailing earnings, 39 times forward earnings, and 27 times enterprise value divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation (EV/EBITDA) — above the analyst median price target of approximately $304 and at the top of the entire contractor peer group (Quanta Services at roughly 25 times EV/EBITDA, MasTec at roughly 26 times, Primoris at roughly 16 times). The market has fully priced a flawless multi-year build-out; the marginal surprise now must be even better than the already-record results to drive further gains. The first crack is already visible: the book-to-bill ratio (new orders booked divided by revenue recognised) is running below 1.0 times on a trailing basis — the backlog grew 7.7% year-over-year while revenue grew 20% — meaning the order book is growing slower than revenue is being consumed. Management's defence is that large projects will book in future quarters, a promise rather than a signed contract. The Q1 gross margin of 13.4% is flattered by project close-outs and favourable change orders (management's own words); the durable run-rate is the lower guidance bands, not 13.4%. The competitive moat is narrow — electrical construction is fragmented, low-switching-cost, and re-bid project by project; new data-center entrants are appearing, though margin pressure is 'not yet' visible. The thesis has no idiosyncratic resolution driver — it rests entirely on the same AI-capital-spending and electrification wave that the rest of the scale-out basket depends on, so it offers zero diversification and full correlation to any hyperscaler capital-expenditure pullback. The risk is not bankruptcy or secular decline; it is a high-multiple de-rating on any growth or margin wobble, or a single soft backlog print. The bear case firms up if book-to-bill stays below 1.0 times for another quarter, gross margin reverts toward the mid-range of the guidance band, or a hyperscaler capital-expenditure guide-down hits the whole peer group.
sop_bull: The right valuation framework for a single-economics contractor is whole-company intrinsic value rather than a sum-of-the-parts analysis (valuing each business line separately), because the T&D and C&I segments share labour, bonding capacity, and management and are not separately monetisable. For fiscal 2026, assume revenue of approximately $4.1 billion; at a durable EBITDA margin of roughly 7.5% (consistent with the raised guidance), that produces approximately $307 million of EBITDA. Applying a bull-case multiple of 22 times EV/EBITDA — already a rich premium to the peer group, capped here because MYR's smaller scale and narrower moat do not justify matching Quanta's 25-times multiple — yields an enterprise value of approximately $6.76 billion; add $154 million in net cash to reach an equity value of roughly $6.9 billion divided by 15.57 million shares, or approximately $443 per share. An earnings cross-check using fiscal 2026 estimated earnings per share of roughly $9.47 times a generous 38-times forward multiple yields approximately $360 per share. The EV/EBITDA path is more generous because it capitalises the margin inflection. Bull-case fair value: approximately $443 to $460 per share — roughly the current price, meaning the bull case is essentially 'the stock is fairly valued for a perfect build-out,' not undervalued.
sop_bear: Using the same whole-company intrinsic framework but normalising margins and applying a de-rating: fiscal 2026 revenue of approximately $4.1 billion at a durable EBITDA margin of roughly 6.5% (stripping the Q1 close-out flatter) produces approximately $267 million of EBITDA. Applying a 16-times EV/EBITDA multiple — appropriate for the peer-group value name, Primoris, once the growth premium compresses on a soft backlog print or capital-spending scare — yields an enterprise value of approximately $4.27 billion; add $154 million net cash to reach equity of roughly $4.42 billion divided by 15.57 million shares, or approximately $284 per share. An earnings cross-check at 26 times forward earnings yields approximately $246 per share. Bear-case range: approximately $246 to $284 per share — a 38% to 47% drawdown from $461, landing right around the analyst median target of roughly $304 on the gentler path.
sop_breakdown: Base-case whole-company intrinsic value: fiscal 2026 revenue of approximately $4.1 billion times a durable EBITDA margin of roughly 7.0% equals approximately $287 million of EBITDA. Applying an 18-to-19-times EV/EBITDA multiple — a justified premium to Primoris's 16 times but a discount to Quanta's and MasTec's roughly 25 times, reflecting MYR's smaller scale and narrower moat — produces an enterprise value of roughly $5.3 to $5.45 billion; add $154 million net cash to reach equity of roughly $5.45 to $5.6 billion divided by 15.57 million shares, or approximately $350 to $360 on the upper bound and roughly $305 on a tougher 16-times multiple; the midpoint is approximately $330. An earnings cross-check at roughly 34 times forward earnings (a premium but reasonable multiple for 12% growth with rising margins) yields approximately $322 per share. Both methods converge on roughly $320 to $345. This base case sits 25% to 30% below the current price of approximately $461 and right around the analyst median target of roughly $304 — the AI-capital-spending optionality is already more than priced in.
risk_reward: The risk-reward is unfavourable at roughly $461. Downside to the base case of approximately $330 is negative 28%; downside to the bear case of approximately $284 is negative 38%. Upside to a perfect-execution bull case of roughly $455 is essentially zero. The skew is approximately negative 38% on the downside versus zero upside, meaning you are paying full price for a flawless decade of build-out with a backlog (book-to-bill below 1.0 times) that is already whispering caution.
peer_comps_note:
The true peers are listed electrical and infrastructure construction contractors levered to grid and data-center capital spending, verified as of June 2026. Quanta Services trades at roughly 25 times EV/EBITDA — the scale leader with the broadest grid, renewables, and data-center exposure. MasTec trades at roughly 26 times EV/EBITDA and roughly 40 times forward earnings — communications and power delivery, the highest growth multiple in the group. Primoris trades at roughly 16 times EV/EBITDA and roughly 27 times forward earnings — the peer-group value name. MYR Group itself trades at roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA, roughly 50 times trailing earnings, and roughly 39 times forward earnings.

On valuation, MYR Group is the most expensive on EV/EBITDA (27 times versus the cohort range of 16 to 26 times) and second-most on forward earnings — a meaningful de-rating risk. On growth, MYR's roughly 12% revenue and roughly 26% earnings-per-share guidance is solid but below MasTec's; mid-pack. On margins, the recovered 13.4% gross margin and roughly 7% EBITDA margin are good and improving but structurally thinner than Quanta's at scale; the inflection, not the level, is the story. On returns, MYR's return on invested capital of roughly 15.5% and return on equity of roughly 22.7% are strong and competitive within the group. On AI-capital-spending exposure, MYR is a focused, high-purity play (data-center starts up 100%, though data centers are only roughly 6% of revenue today; the bulk is grid T&D, which is the same scale-out wave) — comparable purity to Quanta, less diversified than MasTec.

Net: MYR Group ranks best or tied on returns, margin trajectory, and balance sheet, but worst on valuation — it is the most expensive way to own the narrowest-moat layer of the contractor group. Primoris at 16 times is the value-relative alternative within the same investment thesis.
role_in_scaleout: MYR Group is the physical electrical contractor that energises grid interconnections (high-voltage transmission lines and substations) and wires mission-critical data halls — the literal labour that turns a hyperscaler's capital expenditure into a powered facility. Demand is highly durable through the build-out decade because grid modernisation and electrification underinvestment is structural and constrained by labour and materials, but the thesis is fully correlated to the durability of AI capital spending with no idiosyncratic driver.
consensus_delta:
There is no favourable disagreement with the market here — the sell-side is not looking the wrong way; if anything it is more bullish than the fundamentals warrant. At roughly $461 the stock trades above the analyst median price target of roughly $304 and above even the high target of roughly $332, meaning the market has run past the sell-side's already-optimistic models. Sell-side consensus assumes the raised 12% revenue and raised-margin-band guidance holds and keeps ratcheting; that the close-out-flattered 13.4% gross margin is a durable new floor rather than a Q1 peak; and that large transmission awards convert to backlog on schedule despite a trailing book-to-bill already below 1.0 times.

The market disagreement runs against a buyer here: the mispricing is that a strong-but-cyclical contractor with a narrow moat is priced at roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA — the top of a peer group whose value name trades at 16 times. The implicit wrong assumption is that MYR deserves a premium-to-peer multiple; on a narrower moat and smaller scale than Quanta, it arguably deserves a discount. The pain trade is downward (de-rating), not upward.
valley_of_death: There is no solvency or cash-burn risk — the balance sheet is pristine (0.04 times leverage, $163 million cash, $230 million trailing-twelve-month free cash flow), so there is no maturity wall or liquidity crunch. The near-term risk window (roughly three to nine months) is purely one of multiple compression and disappointed expectations: (1) a Q2 or Q3 2026 earnings print where the book-to-bill ratio stays below 1.0 times or backlog growth decelerates further would crack the 'decade of visibility' narrative on a 27-times multiple; (2) gross margin reverting from 13.4% toward the mid-range of the operating guidance band as close-out tailwinds fade reads as a miss even though it is simply normalisation; (3) the chief financial officer's flagged drift in days-sales-outstanding (from the mid-50s to the low-60s) pressures reported free cash flow as the master-service-agreement revenue mix increases; (4) the dominant systemic risk is a hyperscaler AI-capital-expenditure guide-down — the macro regime document identifies this as roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings growth and the single load-bearing wall — which would de-rate the entire scale-out peer group simultaneously, and MYR at the top of the valuation cohort would fall hardest. The risky window is a de-rating event, not a default.
positioning_washout: There is no dividend-cut or yield-base forced-selling dynamic — MYR pays no meaningful dividend and the shareholder base is growth and momentum capital, not income investors. The mechanical risk runs in the opposite direction: after a 176% gain over 52 weeks, the shareholder base is crowded with momentum and AI-thematic capital that bought the breakout rather than the value. That base is reflexive — it will liquidate on the first disappointing backlog or margin print, or on any cohort-wide AI-capital-spending de-grossing, because there is no valuation floor to catch it (the stock already trades above the analyst median target). This is a momentum-unwind risk rather than a forced-passive-seller catalyst, but it makes the downside path faster and more violent than the fundamentals alone would imply.
forcing_function: There is no positive catalyst that forces a re-rating pending — the re-rating catalyst (the fiscal 2024 to fiscal 2025 to Q1 2026 margin recovery and the AI and grid demand reveal) has already fired, and the 176% gain is the re-rating. The dated events ahead are risk-skewed: the Q2 2026 earnings call (approximately late July 2026), where book-to-bill and margin durability get re-tested, and any hyperscaler capital-expenditure guidance update, which is the macro tripwire. The next catalyst that forces a re-rating for this stock is a downside one — a soft backlog print or a peer-group capital-spending scare — not an upside one.
moderator_conclusion:
MYR Group is a textbook case of a great business at a bad price, and the macro regime makes that worse, not better. The analyst who prepared the forensic dossier (credibility 4 of 5, trajectory strengthening, moat narrow but real, moat trend stable, secular threat manageable) is right that the fundamentals are excellent and corroborated: net income quadrupled off the fiscal 2024 trough, margins inflected, the backlog is at a record, the balance sheet is pristine, return on invested capital is above the cost of capital and rising, and management is credible and disciplined. But the load-bearing catalyst — the recovery and AI-grid demand reveal — has already fired, and the 176% gain over 52 weeks is the re-rating.

Applying the catalyst-watch regime framework: the regime penalises already-fired catalysts and 'interest-rate rescue' theses, and demands that a resolution driver be independent of shared macro factors. MYR fails both tests — its catalyst is spent and its entire thesis hinges on the single dominant shared macro factor (AI capital-spending durability) that the regime flags as approximately 40% of 2026 earnings growth and the top book-concentration risk.

At roughly $461 the stock trades at roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA — the top of the Quanta, MasTec, and Primoris peer group versus Primoris's 16 times — and above the analyst median target of roughly $304. The base-case intrinsic value is roughly $320 to $345; the bear case is roughly $284. That is roughly 28% downside to base and roughly 38% to the bear, against essentially zero upside to a perfect-execution bull case of roughly $455. The risk-reward is negatively skewed, and the first hairline crack — book-to-bill below 1.0 times — is already in the data. The AI scale-out optionality is not just priced in; it is over-priced in.

Verdict: pass for now, conviction 2. This is the most expensive way to own the narrowest-moat layer of the grid contractor group, with a spent catalyst and full correlation to the basket's shared macro tail. If forced to own grid-construction exposure, Primoris at 16 times is the value-relative expression and Quanta (the scale leader) the quality-relative one. MYR Group only becomes interesting on a greater-than-30% de-rating toward the high-$200s to low-$300s, where the intrinsic value and the durable-margin floor actually support the price. Valuation conviction is also 2: stripping the catalyst and regime overlay entirely, the valuation still sits 25% to 30% above intrinsic fair value — there is no value cushion, only quality at a premium. Wait for the wash-out.
interrogator_dossier:
MYR Group (MYRG) — Forensic Dossier

Forensic review evaluated within the AI scale-out build-out (scale-out Basket B — Grid). Source: web (Q1 2026 earnings call transcript, Q4 and full-year 2025, full-year 2024 results, live fundamentals June 2026).

MYR Group is a US and Canada electrical-construction contractor operating in two segments: Transmission and Distribution (T&D) and Commercial and Industrial (C&I). It is a labour-and-execution business that bids, builds, and closes out high-voltage lines, substations, and mission-critical electrical fit-outs for data centres, water and wastewater facilities, semiconductor fabs, and healthcare campuses. Its role in the AI build-out is as the physical electrical contractor that energises grid interconnections and wires data halls.

Narrative Arc

The story over the past roughly six quarters is a margin-recovery and structural-demand-inflection arc that has dramatically strengthened. Fiscal 2024 was the trough (net income $30.3 million, earnings per share $1.83, gross margin 8.6%; management framing was defensive — 'de-risk the book' after problematic clean-energy and large C&I projects). Fiscal 2025 was the turn (revenue $3.66 billion, net income $118.4 million up 291%, earnings per share $7.53, gross margin 11.6%; the pivot from 'fixing the book' to 'structural demand is here'). Q1 2026 confirmed the inflection (record revenue $1.0 billion up 20%, net income $46.8 million, earnings per share $2.99, gross margin 13.4%, EBITDA $81.5 million, record backlog $2.84 billion; full-year revenue guidance raised from 10% to 12% and both segment operating-margin bands raised). New demand drivers were introduced and quantified: data-centre construction starts up nearly 100% year-over-year (CEO Egan), water and wastewater as the strongest C&I growth segment, and large transmission visibility extending into 2027 and beyond. The CEO anchored T&D to a decade-long nationwide build rather than the localised 2013–15 CREZ boom. Nothing material dropped; the additions are accretive and corroborated.

Claims versus Financials (Forensic)

Record revenue, backlog, and EBITDA: verified (real records, not adjusted). Structurally higher margins: verified but partly cyclical — management itself flagged 'favourable change orders' and 'project close-outs,' so part of Q1's 13.4% gross margin is non-repeatable; the structural floor is the raised guidance band (T&D 8–11% operating margin, C&I 6–9%), not 13.4%. Structural multi-year demand: verified on direction, but the backlog grew only 7.7% year-over-year while revenue grew 20%, implying a trailing book-to-bill below 1.0 times — a yellow flag that bulls tend to gloss over. Pristine balance sheet and optionality: verified (funded debt $9 million, cash $163 million, 0.04 times leverage, $230 million trailing-twelve-month free cash flow — the cleanest claim in the file). Strong returns: verified (return on equity roughly 22.7%, three-year return on invested capital roughly 15.5%, both above the cost of capital and rising). In summary, the narrative is roughly matched by the numbers, with one honest self-disclosed caveat: Q1 margins were flattered by close-outs.

Tone and Confidence Trajectory — Rising

Fiscal 2024 was defensive ('de-risk,' 'selectivity,' apologising for inefficiencies). Fiscal 2025 was 'recovery on track.' Q1 2026 is clearly offensive: guidance raised twice (revenue and both margin bands), the CEO invoking a decade-long nationwide transmission build, and the chief operating officer quantifying data-centre starts up 100%. Yet disciplined hedges were retained: 'we continue to be selective on the larger projects,' 'we don't want to be the first in,' 'we haven't seen margin pressure from these new entrants.' Capital expenditure was guided above historical norms (3% of revenue), signalling belief in durable demand. The shift from defensive to offensive is unambiguous and evidence-backed.

Guidance Credibility — Medium to High (4 of 5)

The track record is short and asymmetric: Q4 2024 was a miss on both earnings per share and revenue, so reliability cannot be scored 'high' on a clean beat streak. But the four-quarter recovery culminating in a raised fiscal 2026 guidance has been delivered or exceeded each quarter through Q1 2026. The chief financial officer pre-disclosed the drift in days-sales-outstanding (from the mid-50s to the low-60s as the master-service-agreement revenue mix increases) — a credibility green flag. Held back from a 5 of 5 only by the recency of the fiscal 2024 miss and the close-out-flattered Q1 margin.

Analyst Pressure Points

Sustainability of the 13.4% gross margin (the contested variable — new durable floor or Q1 close-out peak?); book-to-bill and backlog conversion (why the backlog grew only 7.7% while revenue grew 20%; management deflects to 'future quarters' and revenue booking 'in 2027 or later' — plausible but unverified until awards actually book); new data-centre competition (the CEO's 'no margin pressure yet' is a claim to watch); and labour and materials availability (management flagged that clients worry about where they get materials and how they secure labour — the real governor on the build-out, a two-sided constraint).

Red Flags and Green Flags

Green: net-cash balance sheet (0.04 times leverage), $230 million trailing-twelve-month free cash flow; broad-based margin recovery across both segments; return on equity roughly 22.7% and return on invested capital roughly 15.5%, both above the cost of capital and rising; management pre-disclosing days-sales-outstanding friction; quantified secular demand (data-centre starts up 100%, decade-long transmission build, water and wastewater growth).

Red: valuation detached from fundamentals (up 176% over 52 weeks to roughly $461; roughly 50 times trailing earnings, roughly 39 times forward earnings, roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA — above the analyst median target of roughly $304 and at the top of the peer group versus Primoris at 16 times); book-to-bill below 1.0 times trailing (order book growing slower than revenue is consumed); Q1 gross margin flattered by close-outs and change orders (the durable margin is the lower guidance band); single-factor dependence on AI-capital-spending and electrification durability shared with the rest of scale-out Basket B.

Hidden Signals

The terminology shift from 'de-risk the book' in 2024 to 'selective, don't want to be first in' in 2026 reflects the same discipline, now exercised from a position of strength (demand-rationing) rather than damage control. Capital expenditure guided above historical norms (3% of revenue, T&D-weighted) signals belief that demand is durable enough to own capacity. Acquisition language reactivated ('high-quality companies out there'; cash available for 'acquisitions or buybacks'). Master service agreements now roughly 70% of T&D revenue — a quietly important improvement in the recurring quality of earnings, at the cost of higher days-sales-outstanding.

Capital-Allocation Assessment (for the portfolio director)

Trajectory: strengthening. The business is unambiguously improving — margins, returns, backlog, balance sheet, and demand visibility all moved in the right direction, corroborated by hard numbers and credible, pre-disclosing management. The single thing the director must weigh is the gap between the business (excellent, strengthening) and the stock (already priced for perfection at roughly 50 times trailing earnings and 27 times EV/EBITDA, up 176% over the year, and above the analyst median target and the top of the peer group). The AI-capital-spending optionality is largely already in the price; the marginal surprise must be even stronger than the already-record results to drive further expansion — and a book-to-bill below 1.0 times is the first hairline crack. Moat: scale cost, execution reputation, and bonding and skilled-labour intangibles, but electrical construction is fragmented, low-switching-cost, and re-bid project by project; new entrants are appearing — narrow, stable. Secular threat: MYR Group is a winner of electrification and grid build-out, not a victim; the terminal risk is cyclical (a capital-expenditure digestion air pocket), not structural impairment — AI does not displace the physical act of energising high-voltage lines. Secular threat: none to manageable. Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: The bear case's load-bearing pillar — that the stock at roughly $461 trades above the analyst median price target of roughly $304 and above even the high target of roughly $332, so sell-side consensus is more bearish than the price and the disagreement with the market runs against the buyer — is false on current data. As of June 2026, the consensus average price target is roughly $442 to $455 (median roughly $459, per stockanalysis.com, based on 7 analysts), with the high target at roughly $503 to $564. Multiple analysts raised targets sharply in May 2026: Stifel from $351 to $503, Clear Street from $400 to $500, and Baird from $373 to $450. The '$304 median and $332 high' that the moderator's original framing leaned on is a stale, low-coverage snapshot superseded by the post-Q1 2026 upward re-rating of sell-side targets. Current analyst targets bracket and exceed the $461 price rather than sitting roughly 33% below it, so the central de-rating and 'over-priced versus the street' argument does not hold against a current read of consensus. The underlying financials all verify (Q1 2026 revenue $1,000.4 million up 20.0% year-over-year, net income $46.8 million, diluted earnings per share $2.99, gross margin 13.4%, EBITDA $81.5 million, record backlog $2.84 billion comprising $981 million T&D and $1.86 billion C&I, cash $163 million, total debt $9.4 million, 0.04 times leverage, 15.57 million shares outstanding, price $461.10 as of 18 June 2026) — the correction targets the bear's valuation framing, not the numbers.
skeptic_corrections: Replace the stale '$304 median and $332 high' analyst-target anchor with current consensus (average roughly $442 to $455, median roughly $459, high roughly $503 to $564; Stifel $503, Clear Street $500, Baird $450, all raised in May 2026). The 'priced above the street' claim must be dropped. The residual bear points that do verify and should be retained are: (1) the Q1 13.4% gross margin is partly non-durable — the quarterly filing attributes it to 'favourable change orders and job close-outs' plus net estimate changes of plus 0.8% and plus $8.5 million in operating income, so the durable run-rate is the lower guidance bands; (2) the valuation is genuinely rich and at the top of the peer group (roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA versus Primoris at roughly 16 times) and the re-rating catalyst has largely fired (up 176% over 52 weeks); and (3) the thesis is single-factor (AI-capital-spending and electrification) with no idiosyncratic resolution driver — full correlation to the rest of scale-out Basket B. These factors cap conviction but are not disqualifiers.
skeptic_evidence: stockanalysis.com/stocks/myrg/forecast/ (fetched 2026-06-22): MYRG analyst consensus avg target ~$455, median ~$459.5, high ~$564, low ~$295; 7 analysts, Buy -- i.e. targets bracket/exceed the $461 price, NOT ~$304.
CNN Markets / Benzinga analyst-rating feed (search 2026-06-22): May-2026 target raises -- Stifel $351->$503, Clear Street $400->$500, Baird $373->$450 -- post-Q1-26 upward re-rate of price targets.

MYR Group Q1-2026 8-K / 10-Q via SEC EDGAR (CIK 0000700923, quarter ended 2026-03-31): revenue $1,000.4M (+20.0% YoY), net income $46.8M, diluted EPS $2.99, gross margin 13.4% (from 11.6%), EBITDA $81.5M, record backlog $2.84B ($981M T&D + $1.86B C&I), cash $163M, total debt $9.4M, 0.04x leverage; 10-Q attributes margin to 'favorable change orders and job closeouts' (close-out flatter confirmed).

stockanalysis.com/stocks/myrg/ (fetched 2026-06-22): price $461.10 as of 2026-06-18, market cap $7.18B, 15.57M shares, P/E 50.76, 52wk $165.00-$484.71 -- price/share-count anchors in the debate file verified.

scale.rationale: Skeptic verdict REFUTED — the bear's load-bearing '$304 median target' anchor is FALSE (current consensus ~$442-455, targets bracket/exceed the price), which cuts FOR the name. But a REFUTED verdict forces TACTICAL/PASS and a sub-35 score regardless of the underlying franchise. The residual VERIFIED bear points stand: 13.4% GM partly non-durable (favorable change-orders/closeouts), book-to-bill <1.0x (decelerating backlog), ~27x EV/EBITDA top-of-cohort, base ~$330 ~28% below spot, full single-factor correlation. With B2B<1.0x and the -28% base gap I take TACTICAL at the low end.
scale.demand_durability: Medium — grid-connection labor, but backlog momentum is decelerating (B2B <1.0x).
